segments.title: Historical naval technology disparity examples
segments.question: Are there historical naval battles with a modern-carrier-versus-old-war level of technological disparity?
segments.answerShort: The answer points to Royal Navy counter-piracy work in the 1920s South China Sea, where aircraft from carriers could intervene against junks armed with boarding weapons rather than guns.
segments.summary: A Bruships 240 Q&A asks for real naval technology-disparity examples, and the answer cites 1920s Royal Navy carrier aircraft used in South China Sea counter-piracy.
segments.body: This live Q&A segment captures a clean audience prompt from a long Bruships stream. The answer uses Royal Navy operations against pirates in the South China Sea as a practical example of extreme naval technology mismatch: merchant ships signaled distress, carrier aircraft located junks attempting to board them, and the aircraft's machine guns or bombs changed the encounter. Later live-Q&A review can add further questions from the rest of the stream.

evidence.note: The viewer asks whether any historical naval battle had the same level of technological disparity as modern ships inserted into older wars.
The answer identifies South China Sea piracy cases where carrier aircraft confronted junks whose crews often lacked guns.
The transcript explains the Royal Navy counter-piracy pattern: listen for a flare, send aircraft toward the merchant ship, and deter or attack boarders.
